messages.content: When did development begin for Pokemon X and Y

Development for Pokémon X and Y began in 2010. These games were officially announced by Nintendo on January 8, 2013, and were released worldwide for the Nintendo 3DS on October 12, 2013. They were the first games in the series to feature fully 3D polygonal graphics, and introduced players to the Kalos region, inspired by France, along with a new generation of Pokémon. Game Freak, the developer of the Pokémon games, introduced various new features with X and Y, including Mega Evolution and the Fairy type.
When did development begin for Pokemon Black and White

Development for Pokémon Black and White began immediately after the completion of Pokémon Platinum, which would place the start of Black and White's development around late 2008. Junichi Masuda, the director of Pokémon Black and White, mentioned in an interview that development took about four years. Pokémon Black and White were first released in Japan in September 2010 and then rolled out to other regions in March 2011. These games introduced the Unova region and were the first games in the fifth generation of the Pokémon video game series.
When did development begin for Pokemon Sun and Moon

Development for Pokémon Sun and Moon began around late 2013, following the release of Pokémon X and Y. The games were developed by Game Freak and published by The Pokémon Company and Nintendo for the Nintendo 3DS. Sun and Moon are the first installments in the seventh generation of Pokémon games and were officially announced on the franchise's 20th anniversary, February 27, 2016.

These titles introduced the Hawaii-inspired Alola region and brought various new features into the franchise, including Alolan forms of pre-existing Pokémon, Z-Moves, and the removal of traditional gyms. Pokémon Sun and Moon were released worldwide on November 18, 2016, with a few European countries receiving the games slightly later, on November 23, 2016.
When did development begin for Pokemon Sword and Shield

Development for Pokémon Sword and Shield began after the completion of Pokémon Sun and Moon, which was released in November 2016. Since the games were announced on February 27, 2019, during a Pokémon Direct presentation, the development likely started in 2017 or even earlier. Sword and Shield were released worldwide on November 15, 2019, and were developed by Game Freak for the Nintendo Switch. They are the first main series games in the eighth generation of the Pokémon franchise and introduced players to the Galar region, which is inspired by Great Britain. These games introduced features like Dynamax and Gigantamax transformations, the Wild Area, and also discarded some traditional elements such as Mega Evolutions and Z-Moves from previous generations.
When did development begin for Pokemon Scarlet and Violet

Specific details about the exact start date of development for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet have not been officially disclosed. However, in general, Pokémon games typically enter development several years before their release. Considering that Pokémon Scarlet and Violet were announced on Pokémon Day, February 27, 2022, and released on November 18, 2022, it is likely that the development began at least 2-3 years prior to the announcement, which could place the beginning of development around 2019 or 2020.

These games are the first entries in the ninth generation of the Pokémon series and include a number of new features and mechanics, such as a fully open-world design and the "Terastal" phenomenon that allows Pokémon to gain new forms and powers. Pokémon Scarlet and Violet are developed by Game Freak for the Nintendo Switch and are set in a region inspired by the Iberian Peninsula.
When did development begin for Pokemon Black 2 and White 2

Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 were officially announced in February 2012, and the games were released in June 2012 for Japan and later in October 2012 for North America and Europe. While the exact start date of their development has not been officially detailed, it's reasonable to infer that development began shortly after the release of Pokémon Black and White, which was in September 2010 in Japan and in March 2011 internationally.

Given the typical development cycle of Pokémon games and the fact that Black 2 and White 2 are direct sequels, utilizing the same game engine and many assets from their predecessors, the development time was likely shorter than that of a brand-new generation. It is common for Game Freak to begin conceptualizing and working on new titles while the previous games are still being finalized or shortly after their release. Therefore, it's plausible that the development of Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 could have started as early as late 2010 or 2011. These assumptions are based on standard industry practices and the timelines between game releases.
When did development begin for Pokemon Diamond and Pearl

Pokémon Diamond and Pearl were in development for several years before their release. While there isn't an exact publicly disclosed start date for the development of these games, they were released in Japan on September 28, 2006, and in the United States on April 22, 2007.

Junichi Masuda, the game director and composer for Diamond and Pearl, revealed in interviews that the games took approximately three and a half years to develop. Given this information, it can be estimated that the development of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl likely began sometime in 2002 or early 2003. This development period encompasses the transition to the Nintendo DS platform, which offered new technical opportunities and challenges for Game Freak, such as the implementation of Wi-Fi connectivity for online battles and trading. Diamond and Pearl were the first games in the series released for the Nintendo DS and marked the beginning of the fourth generation of Pokémon games.
When did development begin for Pokemon Ruby and Sapphire

Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, which were the first games in the series’ third generation and introduced players to the Hoenn region, began development in 2000, shortly after the completion of Pokémon Gold and Silver for the Game Boy Color. The games were developed over a span of three years, with a team that peaked at around 50 developers.

Junichi Masuda, the director of Ruby and Sapphire, has discussed in various interviews the challenge they faced in moving from the Game Boy Color to the new Game Boy Advance hardware, which involved adapting to the new technical capabilities and widescreen format.

Given the release dates of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire—November 2002 in Japan and March 2003 in North America—it can be inferred that the development process covered the period of 2000 to 2002 before finalizing the product for release.
When did development begin for Pokemon Gold and Silver

The development of Pokémon Gold and Silver began around late 1997 or early 1998, following the release of Pokémon Red and Green (Blue in international versions) in 1996. These games took around three years to develop and posed a significant challenge due to the limitations of the Game Boy hardware and the initial absence of series creator Satoshi Tajiri, who was less involved in the early stages of development due to health issues.

One of the most notable aspects of Gold and Silver's development was the contributions of Satoru Iwata, who was then the president of HAL Laboratory. Iwata was instrumental in creating compression tools that allowed the team to fit the Johto region, as well as the entirety of the Kanto region from the first games, into the Game Boy cartridge.

Pokémon Gold and Silver were first released in Japan on November 21, 1999, followed by North American and Australian releases in October 2000 and European release in April 2001. These titles introduced the second generation of Pokémon and were lauded for their innovations, including a real-time clock with day/night cycles, breeding mechanics, and two new Pokémon types (Dark and Steel).
When did development begin for Pokemon Red and Green

Pokémon Red and Green, the games that started the iconic Pokémon series, had a particularly long and challenging development period. Development began in 1990 by the company that would become known as Game Freak, led by Satoshi Tajiri and <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>. Tajiri was inspired by his childhood interest in collecting creatures, and with the advent of the Game Boy and its link cable feature, he envisioned a game where creatures could be traded between players.

The development process for these games was fraught with financial difficulties, and it took approximately six years to complete. The extended development time was partly due to the team's inexperience and the ambitious nature of the project. Game Freak nearly faced bankruptcy during this period, and many of the staff went unpaid in the final stretch of the project. Satoshi Tajiri continued to work on the game even during those challenging times, fueled by his passion for the concept.

Nintendo's Shigeru Miyamoto mentored the project, helping the team refine the games and get them ready for launch. In addition, composer Junichi Masuda worked on the entire game soundtrack using a PC-88, a Japanese home computer.

Pokémon Red and Green were eventually released for the Game Boy in Japan on February 27, 1996. Due to the games' success in Japan, they were later localized and released as Pokémon Red and Blue in North America in 1998 and in Europe in 1999, helping to spark the international Pokémon phenomenon.
When did pre-production begin for the Pokemon Best Wishes Anime

Pre-production for the Pokémon anime series titled "Best Wishes!" in Japan, known as "Pokémon: Black & White" in English-speaking territories, likely began well in advance of its Japanese television premiere date, which was September 23, 2010. Pre-production involves planning the arc of the series, scriptwriting, storyboarding, casting, and other preparatory work before full-scale production begins.

Generally, pre-production for an anime series can start more than a year before the actual airing of the first episode, especially for an established franchise like Pokémon, where scheduling and coordination with the related video game releases are crucial. This would suggest that pre-production for the "Best Wishes!" season could have started as early as 2009 or even before, aligning with the development and eventual release of the Pokémon Black and White video games, which were closely tied to the new series in terms of setting and Pokémon species showcased.

Though there are no official public statements specifying the exact dates when pre-production began, this timeline would make sense within the context of the industry and the planning required for a synchronized multimedia release strategy that the Pokémon franchise is known for.
When did development begin for Pokemon Diamond and Pearl

The development of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl began after the completion of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, which were released for the Game Boy Advance in 2002 in Japan and 2003 internationally. Since Diamond and Pearl were released in 2006 in Japan and 2007 elsewhere, it's likely that the development of these titles started at least two to three years prior to their release, around 2003 or 2004.

Junichi Masuda, the director of many Pokémon games including Diamond and Pearl, has spoken in interviews about the development process. Moving to the Nintendo DS platform posed new challenges and provided opportunities to utilize the system's dual screens and enhanced graphical capabilities. One of the significant advancements in Diamond and Pearl was the ability to connect globally via the Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection, which represented a big leap in the series in terms of multiplayer capabilities.

Considering the complexity of creating games for new hardware and the need to innovate in the Pokémon series, a development time of more than two years is consistent with industry standards and the practices of Game Freak. This would align with the idea of starting the development process shortly after the team completed work on the previous generation of Pokémon games.
When did development start for Yu-Gi-Oh Zexal

"Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal" is part of the larger "Yu-Gi-Oh!" franchise, which includes multiple anime series, manga, and trading card games. "Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal" refers to the anime series which is the fourth main spin-off of the "Yu-Gi-Oh!" franchise. The development of "Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal" would have involved pre-production activities such as storyboarding, scriptwriting, and character design, and these typically begin well before the show's broadcast.

The "Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal" anime first aired in Japan on April 11, 2011. While there is no specific public information about the exact start date of the development for "Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal," it is standard for anime series to begin the pre-production phase at least one year before airing. Given this, the development of "Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal" likely began in 2010 or possibly even earlier.

As for the manga version, which sometimes runs concurrently with the anime's development and release, "Yu-Gi-Oh! Zexal" began serialization in the December 2010 issue of V Jump, a Japanese magazine focused on new manga and card games related to popular anime. The manga's development would have started some time before this serialization date.
When did development begin for Power Rangers Megaforce

"Power Rangers Megaforce" is the twentieth season of the Power Rangers series and premiered on Nickelodeon on February 2, 2013. Unfortunately, specific details regarding the exact start date of the development phase for "Power Rangers Megaforce" are not public information. However, the process of creating a Power Rangers season usually involves several stages, including writing, casting, costume and set design, localization of footage from the Super Sentai series, and production planning.

Given that the series adapts footage from the Super Sentai franchise, with "Megaforce" specifically using footage from "Tensou Sentai Goseiger," development for the Power Rangers adaptation would have begun after the decision was made on which Super Sentai series to adapt. Typically, there is about a year or two gap between the original airing of the Super Sentai series in Japan and the premiere of the Power Rangers adaptation in the United States.

Considering "Tensou Sentai Goseiger" aired in Japan starting in 2010 and "Power Rangers Megaforce" premiered in early 2013, it is reasonable to speculate that the development for "Megaforce" started sometime in 2011 or 2012, allowing enough time for adaptation work, pre-production, production, and post-production before the series debut.
When did development begin for Power Rangers Super Megaforce

"Power Rangers Super Megaforce" is the twenty-first season of the Power Rangers franchise and serves as the second season and continuation of "Power Rangers Megaforce." It debuted on Nickelodeon on February 15, 2014. Since "Super Megaforce" aired directly after "Megaforce," it is likely that the development and planning for it began during or shortly after the production of "Megaforce."

Typically, with back-to-back seasons like "Megaforce" and "Super Megaforce," the planning, scripting, and pre-production activities might overlap with the latter stages of the previous season. This ensures a seamless transition in the storyline and character development, as well as an efficient use of production resources. "Power Rangers Super Megaforce" adapted footage, costumes, and props from the 35th series of the Super Sentai franchise, "Kaizoku Sentai Gokaiger," which originally aired in Japan in 2011.

Considering the airing schedule and the usual production timeline of television series, it is fair to speculate that the development of "Super Megaforce" might have started as early as 2012 or during 2013, to allow time for adaptation decisions, story development, set and costume preparations, casting (if needed), and other essential pre-production processes before filming commenced.
When did development begin for Power Rangers Dino Charge

"Power Rangers Dino Charge" is the twenty-second season of the Power Rangers franchise. It premiered on Nickelodeon on February 7, 2015. The series was adapted from the 37th season of the Super Sentai series called "Zyuden Sentai Kyoryuger," which aired in Japan in 2013.

Though specific details on the start of the development for "Power Rangers Dino Charge" are not publicly disclosed, it's common for production to start at least a year before a show premieres. This includes pre-production activities like writing, casting, adapting the footage from the Super Sentai series, and creating costumes and sets.

Given that "Power Rangers Super Megaforce" aired in 2014, production work for "Dino Charge" would logically follow relatively soon after "Super Megaforce" was well underway or nearly complete to maintain an efficient production schedule. With this in mind, it's reasonable to infer that development for "Power Rangers Dino Charge" likely started sometime in 2013 or 2014. This would have given the production team ample time to complete all the necessary steps to have the show ready for its 2015 debut.
When did development begin for Power Rangers NInja Steel

"Power Rangers Ninja Steel" is the twenty-fourth season of the Power Rangers series, which premiered on January 21, 2017. While there are no specific public records detailing the exact start of development for the series, we can make an educated guess based on the typical production timeline of a Power Rangers season.

The season is based on the 39th season of the Super Sentai series, "Shuriken Sentai Ninninger," which aired in Japan beginning in February 2015. Typically, Power Rangers production teams start working on a new season while the previous season is still airing. This includes initial planning, adaptation decisions, writing, and casting, which would suggest that pre-production activities for "Ninja Steel" likely began around a year prior to its premiere.

Considering this usual timeframe, development for "Power Rangers Ninja Steel" probably started sometime in 2015 or early 2016, allowing sufficient time for pre-production, filming, and post-production before the series premiered in early 2017.
When did development begin for Power Rangers Beast Morphers

"Power Rangers Beast Morphers" is the twenty-sixth season of the Power Rangers franchise, and it premiered on March 2, 2019. It was originally announced in February 2018 that "Power Rangers Beast Morphers" would air in 2019, making it the first season under the franchise's new owner, Hasbro, after acquiring it from Saban Brands in May 2018.

While the exact date that development began is not public information, we can estimate that pre-production activities likely commenced shortly after the acquisition, with preliminary planning even possibly beginning before the announcement. Considering the show premiered in early 2019, it's reasonable to assume that development would have started at some point in 2018.

"Beast Morphers" is adapted from the 36th season of the Super Sentai series, "Tokumei Sentai Go-Busters," which originally aired in Japan in 2012. This adaptation choice was notable since "Go-Busters" was initially skipped over for earlier seasons of Power Rangers in favor of adapting later Super Sentai series.

In the case of creating a new season of Power Rangers, development includes adapting the Japanese footage to fit the new narrative, writing scripts, designing new elements, casting, filming, and post-production work. Given the normal timescales associated with such work, and the fact that Hasbro would have wanted to solidify its vision for the series, development likely started early on in 2018 to allow for a seamless transition and ensure everything was ready for the 2019 launch.
When did development begin for Power Rangers Dino Fury

"Power Rangers Dino Fury" is the twenty-eighth season of the Power Rangers franchise and premiered on February 20, 2021. As with other Power Rangers series, there is usually a significant lead time for development, which includes scriptwriting, casting, and production planning, as well as the process of adapting the chosen Super Sentai series to a Western audience.

For "Dino Fury," the Super Sentai series being adapted is "Kishiryu Sentai Ryusoulger," which originally aired in Japan starting in March 2019. Considering the time needed for localization and production, it's likely that development for "Dino Fury" began at least one to two years prior to its premiere.

While there is no public information detailing the exact start date of the development process for "Power Rangers Dino Fury," it's reasonable to speculate that preliminary work began sometime in 2019, following the airing of the original Japanese source material and based on the show's premiere date in 2021. This would be in line with the typical timing for developing new seasons of the Power Rangers series.

"Power Rangers Dino Fury" is notable for being the first season fully produced under the ownership of Hasbro, which acquired the franchise from Saban Brands in 2018. As such, Hasbro's planning process and production schedule for this new installment may have been formulated to both refresh the brand and align with its broader strategic goals for the franchise.
When did development begin for Power Rangers RPM

"Power Rangers RPM" is the seventeenth season of the Power Rangers franchise, which debuted on March 7, 2009. Unfortunately, the specific start date for the development of "Power Rangers RPM" is not publicly available, but generally, a Power Rangers series goes through a varied development timeline that is initiated well before the show's premiere date.

Taking into account the general timeline of a TV series production, pre-production activities such as writing, planning, casting, and adapting the corresponding Super Sentai footage typically begin at least a year in advance. For "Power Rangers RPM," this would likely have been sometime in 2007 or early 2008, given that it adapts footage from the Super Sentai series "Engine Sentai Go-Onger," which aired in Japan from February 2008 to February 2009.

It's worth noting that "Power Rangers RPM" was believed to be the potential final season at the time due to the franchise's uncertain future after Disney decided to end production of new episodes. Despite this, "RPM" received praise for its writing and story, which deviated from the franchise's typical formula and presented a more serious and post-apocalyptic setting. After "RPM," Saban Brands re-acquired the Power Rangers franchise in 2010, leading to the production of "Power Rangers Samurai" in 2011, which continued the series.
When did pre-production begin for Power Rangers RPM

Pre-production for "Power Rangers RPM" would have included work on scripts, story arcs, set design, costumes, casting, and coordinating the adaptation of the source footage from "Engine Sentai Go-Onger," the Super Sentai series from which "RPM" was adapted. Given that "Power Rangers RPM" premiered in March 2009, pre-production likely started at least several months to a year prior.

"Power Rangers RPM" was unique due to its darker tone and more serious narrative. This required a more thoughtful approach during the pre-production phase to ensure the show's different mood and style were properly captured. Additionally, the series faced an unusual situation because it was produced during a time when it was presumed to be the final season of Power Rangers under Disney's ownership.

With this in mind, it's reasonable to estimate that pre-production for "Power Rangers RPM" would have commenced sometime in late 2007 or early 2008 to facilitate a well-developed storyline and production design, leading up to the early 2009 premiere. However, without specific details from production records, this estimate is based on standard industry timelines for television production.
When did Saban buy the rights to the Zyuranger footage

Saban Entertainment acquired the rights to use footage from "Kyoryu Sentai Zyuranger," the 16th season of the Super Sentai series, which became the basis for the first season of "Mighty Morphin Power Rangers." This acquisition took place in the early 1990s.

Haim Saban, the founder of Saban Entertainment, reportedly came across the Super Sentai series while in Japan and saw potential in adapting the show for Western audiences. After a lengthy process of pitching the idea to various networks and securing the rights, Saban Entertainment was able to create a pilot in 1992. The success of the pilot eventually led to the start of the Power Rangers franchise.

"Mighty Morphin Power Rangers" debuted on American television on August 28, 1993, with much of the action sequences and the iconic costumed heroes drawn directly from the "Zyuranger" footage. The exact year Saban obtained the rights is not specifically detailed in public records, but considering the timeline of the pilot and the debut of the show, the rights acquisition occurred sometime in the late 1980s to early 1990s, prior to the pilot's production.

It was this key decision and the overall success of "Mighty Morphin Power Rangers" that led to the longstanding tradition of adapting Super Sentai footage into what has become one of the most iconic franchises in children's television.
When did Saban buy the rights to Gokaiger footage

Saban Brands re-acquired the Power Rangers franchise from Disney in 2010 and quickly announced plans to produce new content. As part of this transition back to Saban Brands, the company would have needed to secure the rights to new Super Sentai footage for future Power Rangers adaptations.

"Kaizoku Sentai Gokaiger" is the 35th season of the Super Sentai series, and it aired in Japan in 2011. Saban Brands utilized footage from "Gokaiger" to produce "Power Rangers Super Megaforce," which is the sequel to "Power Rangers Megaforce" and began airing in 2014.

There is no publicly available information that precisely states when Saban Brands acquired the rights to the "Gokaiger" footage specifically. However, given "Power Rangers Megaforce" aired in 2013, and "Super Megaforce" began airing in 2014, it's likely that the rights to use "Gokaiger" footage were acquired sometime between 2010 and 2012, in preparation for the back-to-back production schedules of these seasons.

It's important to note that acquisitions of this nature might not be publicized, and the licensing deals can include rights to multiple seasons at once, rather than on a per-season basis. This would enable Saban Brands to plan several seasons ahead and allow for a continuous flow in Power Rangers' production and release schedules.
When did Game Freak finish the completion of Pokemon Platinum

Pokémon Platinum, an enhanced version of Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, was completed and released in Japan on September 13, 2008. It was later released in North America on March 22, 2009, in Europe on May 22, 2009, and in Australia on May 14, 2009.

Typically, the completion of a game, known as "going gold," occurs several weeks before the official release date to allow time for manufacturing and distribution. This means that the final version of Pokémon Platinum would have been finished sometime before its initial Japanese release in September 2008. However, the exact date of completion is not typically disclosed to the public and can vary depending on the project's specifics.

The release of Pokémon Platinum brought new features compared to Diamond and Pearl, such as updated graphics, new areas to explore, changes to the game's plot, enhancements to the gameplay, and the addition of a new Battle Frontier, among other improvements.
When did Game Freak finish the completion of Pokemon OmegaRuby and AlphaSapphire

Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the enhanced remakes of the original Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire games, were completed and released in Japan, North America, Australia, and South Korea on November 21, 2014, with the European release following shortly afterward on November 28, 2014.

As is common in the video game industry, the completion of a game, or when the game is considered "gold", usually happens a few weeks before the official release to ensure there is sufficient time for the game to be manufactured, shipped, and stocked by retailers. While the exact completion date for Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire is not publicly known, it would likely have been in late September or October 2014 to meet the November release schedule.

Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire brought the Hoenn region to the Nintendo 3DS with updated graphics, new Mega Evolutions, and the Primal Reversion mechanic for the legendary Pokémon Groudon and Kyogre. The games were well-received by fans and critics alike, praised for their improvements over the original titles while maintaining the charm and appeal of the third generation.
When did Game Freak finish the completion of Pokemon Black and White

Pokémon Black and White were officially released in Japan on September 18, 2010, and they were later launched in North America, Europe, and Australia in March 2011. As with any video game, the completion, or "going gold," would have occurred some time before the first release date to allow for manufacturing and distribution of the game cartridges.

While the specific date when Pokémon Black and White were completed is not publicly disclosed, the games would have been finalized several weeks prior to the Japanese release date. Based on industry standards, this means Game Freak likely completed the games in late August or early September 2010.

The release of Pokémon Black and White introduced the fifth generation of Pokémon and featured a completely new set of Pokémon, a practice not repeated since the first generation, along with a new region inspired by New York City, updated graphics, and a number of gameplay enhancements. They were designed to present a "new beginning" to the series and received praise for their innovations and fresh take on the Pokémon formula.
When did Junichi Masuda visit France

Junichi Masuda, the director of many Pokémon games and one of the founding members of Game Freak, visited France prior to the development of Pokémon X and Y, which began back in 2010. While there is not a specific, publicly disclosed date for his visit, it is known that his trip to France served as an inspiration for the Kalos region in the games.

In an interview, Junichi Masuda mentioned that he traveled to France to take in the country's culture, art, and cities to inform the design and atmosphere of Kalos. Given that Pokémon X and Y were announced in January 2013 and released in October of the same year, his visit likely took place in the early stages of development, around 2010 or shortly thereafter.

The Kalos region in Pokémon X and Y draws many parallels to various locations and cultural aspects of France, including the in-game city of Lumiose City, which is visually reminiscent of Paris and features a structure that strongly resembles the Eiffel Tower.
Which Heisei phase 2 Kamen Rider shows are adaptable for Saban Brands

Saban Brands, the company that owned the Power Rangers franchise and previously adapted Kamen Rider into American shows, had the potential to adapt any Kamen Rider series that it acquired the rights to. However, not every Kamen Rider series from the Heisei era "phase 2" (which started with Kamen Rider W and continued through Kamen Rider Zi-O, marking the end of the Heisei era) was adapted for an American audience. 

The only Kamen Rider series from the Heisei era that Saban Brands adapted was "Kamen Rider Ryuki," which became "Kamen Rider Dragon Knight" and aired in 2009. However, "Dragon Knight" was adapted under Adness Entertainment, rather than being a direct undertaking by Saban Brands.

The Heisei phase 2 Kamen Rider shows include:

1. Kamen Rider W (2009-2010)
2. Kamen Rider OOO (2010-2011)
3. Kamen Rider Fourze (2011-2012)
4. Kamen Rider Wizard (2012-2013)
5. Kamen Rider Gaim (2013-2014)
6. Kamen Rider Drive (2014-2015)
7. Kamen Rider Ghost (2015-2016)
8. Kamen Rider Ex-Aid (2016-2017)
9. Kamen Rider Build (2017-2018)
10. Kamen Rider Zi-O (2018-2019)

Saban Brands, which was re-established in 2010 after Haim Saban purchased the Power Rangers back from Disney, had the potential to evaluate and adapt any of these, but it chose not to. Hypothetically, any of these series could be adaptable if a production company secured the rights and found creative and practical ways to Westernize the stories and characters for a new audience, as the Power Rangers series has successfully done with the Super Sentai footage. 

After Saban Brands sold Power Rangers to Hasbro in 2018, any potential for Saban to adapt further Kamen Rider series presumably transferred away from the company. The choice of which series to adapt would typically be driven by considerations such as similarity to popular Power Rangers themes, perceived marketability of the characters and suits, and the potential for toy sales. 

To date, there have been no subsequent Kamen Rider adaptations following "Kamen Rider Dragon Knight," although the franchise remains popular within its native country, Japan.
Which Heisei Phase 2 Kamen Rider Seasons could saban hypothetically adapt

Hypothetically, Saban Brands could have considered adapting any of the Heisei phase 2 Kamen Rider seasons, as long as they had the rights to do so. Each season had its unique themes, styles, and potential appeal for Western audiences. Below is a brief look at each Heisei phase 2 Kamen Rider series and elements that could be appealing for adaptation:

1. **Kamen Rider W (2009–2010)** - Features a detective theme, with two protagonists who merge into a single Kamen Rider. Its noir-inspired storytelling approach and the theme of duality could translate well to a Western audience.

2. **Kamen Rider OOO (2010–2011)** - Revolves around ancient powers and artifacts, combining themes of history and adventure that could engage viewers who enjoy mythology-based narratives.

3. **Kamen Rider Fourze (2011–2012)** - A high school setting with space travel and a focus on friendship and social connections, making it potentially relatable to a younger audience.

4. **Kamen Rider Wizard (2012–2013)** - Incorporates magic and fantasy elements, which are often popular in Western media. The show dealt with dark themes and had a strong visual style.

5. **Kamen Rider Gaim (2013–2014)** - Features a theme of dancing teams that fight using lock-based devices which summon powers from interdimensional beings. The combination of dance culture and a more mature, war-torn storyline could catch the interest of diverse demographics.

6. **Kamen Rider Drive (2014–2015)** - Cars and high-speed action are at the center of this series, which could be appealing for an audience that enjoys automotive themes and crime-fighting stories.

7. **Kamen Rider Ghost (2015–2016)** - Deals with supernatural themes and the quest for historical artifacts. Ghostly powers and a journey to collect iconic figures' souls could appeal to adventure genre fans.

8. **Kamen Rider Ex-Aid (2016–2017)** - Centers around video games, a universally popular theme that could resonate well with kids and gamers, plus the added aspect of the characters being doctors adds a unique twist.

9. **Kamen Rider Build (2017–2018)** - The concepts of science, alternate realities, and political intrigue are at play here, offering a mix of intellectual appeal and action.

10. **Kamen Rider Zi-O (2018–2019)** - Time travel and the celebration of Kamen Rider's history could be well-received, especially for nostalgic elements and a time-bending narrative.

For any adaptation, the success would depend on the ability to translate the core elements of the series into something that resonates with Western culture and conventions of superhero storytelling—much like how Mighty Morphin Power Rangers adapted its source material. However, after "Kamen Rider Dragon Knight," there hasn't been another American adaptation of a Kamen Rider series, despite the aforementioned series’ potential. 

It's important to note that whether or not a series is deemed suitable for adaptation also heavily depends on business decisions, including estimations of market viability, potential toy sales, and overall brand strategy. As of my knowledge cutoff date in March 2023, no additional Kamen Rider adaptations had been announced or released in the West since "Kamen Rider Dragon Knight."
Which Heisei Phase 2 Kamen Rider Seasons are unadaptable

It's not accurate to say that any Heisei Phase 2 Kamen Rider seasons are inherently "unadaptable," as the potential to adapt a series largely depends on the creativity of the production team and the cultural sensibilities of the target audience. Each Kamen Rider series has unique themes and concepts that could be adapted with the proper approach, localization, and understanding of the target market.

However, certain elements in each series could present challenges for adaptation, such as:

1. **Kamen Rider W** - The detective noir theme is less common in American children's shows, and the series' dual-protagonist transformation gimmick might be harder to market.

2. **Kamen Rider OOO** - The complexity of the O Medals system and the series' connection to ancient creatures might require a significant cultural adaptation for a Western audience.

3. **Kamen Rider Fourze** - Though the high school setting is relatable, the space theme might need careful integration to avoid feeling too far removed from the usual Power Rangers-style action.

4. **Kamen Rider Wizard** - The show's darker tone and heavy emphasis on magic might be difficult to balance with the lighter tone typically preferred in Western children's entertainment.

5. **Kamen Rider Gaim** - The mix of dance teams and ancient samurai themes could potentially clash, and the show's more mature, complex storyline dealing with inter-faction conflicts would need to be adapted with sensitivity to younger audiences.

6. **Kamen Rider Drive** - While cars are universally popular, the series' crime drama style and more mature procedural elements would need to be modified for a younger demographic.

7. **Kamen Rider Ghost** - The supernatural and historical elements might be challenging to adapt while maintaining accuracy and respect for the historical figures depicted.

8. **Kamen Rider Ex-Aid** - The video game theme could be a hit, but balancing gaming with medical drama might require careful storytelling to keep it engaging for kids.

9. **Kamen Rider Build** - The geopolitical and alternate universe elements could be too complex for younger viewers, and the show's dramatic narrative might need adjustment.

10. **Kamen Rider Zi-O** - The time-travel aspect and celebration of Kamen Rider history could be challenging to convey without a rich understanding of the franchise, and the presence of past Kamen Riders might complicate rights and backstory explanations.

Ultimately, adaptability comes down to whether the production company can effectively localize the story for a new audience. Cultural differences, thematic depth, and complexity of the narrative are just some of the factors that can influence the decision. Moreover, a decision to adapt or not to adapt isn't always about content; it could be dictated by market trends, audience demand, the potential for toy sales, and suitability within a network's programming lineup. 

As of the latest updates before my knowledge cutoff in 2023, there have been no official announcements of new Kamen Rider adaptations after "Kamen Rider Dragon Knight." The success of an adaptation is also dependent on how a new series aligns with the adapting company’s overall brand strategy and objectives.
When did Saban buy the rights to the go-busters footage

Saban Brands, after re-acquiring the Power Rangers franchise from Disney in 2010, would have acquired the rights to adapt the "Tokumei Sentai Go-Busters" footage for "Power Rangers Beast Morphers" at some point prior to its production. However, the specific date when Saban Brands acquired these rights has not been publicly disclosed.

"Tokumei Sentai Go-Busters" originally aired in Japan in 2012, and "Power Rangers Beast Morphers" was announced on February 17, 2018, at New York Toy Fair by Hasbro, which had announced the acquisition of Power Rangers from Saban Brands in a deal expected to be finalized later that year.

Given that the announcement for "Power Rangers Beast Morphers" was made in February 2018 with Saban Brands still involved, the rights acquisition would have occurred before this announcement. Production for "Beast Morphers" likely started shortly after Hasbro's announcement, with pre-production activities such as writing, casting, and set construction happening in advance.

Therefore, while the exact date is unknown, it is reasonable to speculate that Saban Brands obtained the rights to "Go-Busters" sometime before early 2018 when Hasbro announced its plans for "Power Rangers Beast Morphers." This season marked a significant shift in the franchise, both in terms of ownership changes and the decision to finally adapt "Go-Busters," which had been previously skipped in favor of adapting "Zyuden Sentai Kyoryuger" into "Power Rangers Dino Charge."
